content: The ratio of minimum wavelengths of Balmer and Paschen series of hydrogen atom will be
explanation:
Minimum wavelength for the Balmer series:

The formula for the minimum wavelength $\lambda_{\min B}$ in the Balmer series is given by:

$ \frac{1}{\lambda_{\min B}} = R \left(\frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{\infty^2}\right) $

Simplifying this, we have:

$ \frac{1}{\lambda_{\min B}} = \frac{R}{4} $

Thus, the minimum wavelength for the Balmer series $\lambda_{\min B}$ is:

$ \lambda_{\min B} = \frac{4}{R} $

Minimum wavelength for the Paschen series:

Similarly, for the Paschen series, the formula for the minimum wavelength $\lambda_{\min P}$ is:

$ \frac{1}{\lambda_{\min P}} = R \left(\frac{1}{3^2} - \frac{1}{\infty^2}\right) $

This simplifies to:

$ \frac{1}{\lambda_{\min P}} = \frac{R}{9} $

So, the minimum wavelength for the Paschen series $\lambda_{\min P}$ is:

$ \lambda_{\min P} = \frac{9}{R} $

Calculating the ratio:

Now, to find the ratio of the two wavelengths $\lambda_{\min B}$ and $\lambda_{\min P}$, we use the formulas derived:

$ \frac{\lambda_{\min B}}{\lambda_{\min P}} = \frac{\frac{4}{R}}{\frac{9}{R}} = \frac{4}{9} $

Therefore, the ratio of the minimum wavelengths of the Balmer to the Paschen series is $4:9$.

content: The activity of a radioactive sample is measured as No counts per minute at $$t=0$$ and $$\mathrm{N}_0 / \mathrm{e}$$ counts per minute at $$t=6 \mathrm{~min}$$. The time (in minutes) at which the activity reduces to half its value is.
The wavelength of $$k_\alpha$$-line characteristic $$X$$-rays emitted by an element is $$0.32 \mathop A\limits^o$$. The wavelength of the $$k_\beta$$-line emitted by the same element will be

A radioactive element $$X$$ convents into another stable element $$Y$$. Half-life of $$X$$ is 2 hrs. Initially only $$X$$ is present. After time $$t$$, the ratio of atoms of $$X$$ and $$Y$$ is found to be $$1: 4$$, then $$t$$ in hours is
explanation:
Let $$N_0=N_0$$ of atoms of $$X$$ at time $$t=0$$.

At $$t=4$$ hrs (two half lives)

$$N_x=\frac{N_0}{4} \text { and } N_y=\frac{3 N_0}{4} \Rightarrow N_x / N_y=1 / 3$$

At $$t=6$$ hrs (three half lives)

$$N_x=\frac{N_0}{8} \text { and } N_y=\frac{7 N_0}{8}, \frac{N_x}{N_y}=\frac{1}{7}$$

The given ratio $$1 / 4$$ lies between $$1 / 3$$ and $$1 / 7$$. Therefore, $$t$$ lies between 4 hrs and 6 hrs.

content: A nuclide at rest emits an $\alpha$-particle. In this process

options.content: $$\alpha$$-particle moves with large velocity and the nucleus remains at rest.
Both $$\alpha$$-particle and nucleus move with equal speed in opposite directions.
Both move in opposite directions except $$\alpha$$-particle with greater speed.
Both move in opposite direction with greater velocity of $$\alpha$$-particle.

explanation:
As $$f_{\text {ext }}=0$$, Hence $$P_s=$$ constant

or $$\mathrm{P}_\alpha+\mathrm{P}_N=$$ constant

Because nuclide was at rest before emission of $$\alpha$$-particle, hence according to law of conservation of momentum,

$$P_\alpha+P_N=$$ constant - 1

Thus, $$\alpha$$-particle and the nucleus move in opposite directions. Further, as $$m_N \gg m_\alpha$$, hence, $$v_\alpha \gg v_N$$

content: The half-life period of a radioactive element $$x$$ is same as the mean life time of another radioactive element $$y$$. Initially, both of them have the same number of atoms. Then,

options.content: $$x$$ and $$y$$ have the same decay rate initially
$$x$$ and $$y$$ decay at the same rate always
$$y$$ will decay at a faster rate than $$x$$
$$x$$ will decay at a faster rate than $$y$$

explanation:
According to question.

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(t_{1 / 2}\right)_x=\left(t_{\text {mean }}\right)_y \\ & \text { or } \quad \frac{0.693}{\lambda_x}=\frac{1}{\lambda_y} \\ & \therefore \quad \lambda_x=0.693 \lambda_y \Rightarrow \lambda_x<\lambda_y \end{aligned}$$

Since, rate of decay $$=\lambda \mathrm{N}$$

Initially, number of atoms $$(N)$$ of both are equal but since $$\lambda_y>\lambda_x$$, therefore $$y$$ will decay at a faster rate than $$x$$.

content: Polonium has a half-life of 140 days. If we take $$20 \mathrm{~g}$$ of polonium initially then the amount of it that remains after 280 days is
explanation:
After $$n$$-half lines quantity of radiatioactive substance left is

$$N=N_0\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t / T_{1 / 2}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Given, } T_{1 / 2} & =140 \text { days, } t=28 \text { days, } \\ N_0 & =20 \mathrm{~g} \\ \therefore \quad \quad N & =20\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{280}{140}}=\frac{20}{4}=5 \mathrm{~g} \end{aligned}$$

content: According to Bohr model of hydrogen atom, only those orbits are permissible which satisfy the condition
explanation:
According to Bohr's model

$$m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}$$

content: The Rutherford scattering experiment proves that an atom consists of

options.content: a sphere of positive charge in which electrons are embedded like seeds of water-melon
a sphere of negative charge in which protons are embedded like seeds of water-melon
a sphere of electron cloud in which the positive charge in placed at the centre of the sphere

explanation: Rutherford's model states that an atom is a sphere of diameter about $$10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$$ whole of its positive charge is concentrated at the centre.
